node_text: Given a limited amount of contest time, each solved problem i costs 5*i minutes. Find the maximum number of first problems that can be solved without exceeding the remaining time.
n is the number of available problems; k is the time already reserved.
Maximum x such that x <= n and the cumulative time fits.
Small n, k within the contest time window.
[{"note": "Paraphrased demo sample omitted; see source URL."}]
Core inequality: 5*x*(x+1)/2 <= 240-k.

node_title: New Year and Hurry - Editorial Full
node_text: The cumulative time is triangular. A binary-search solution checks whether a candidate x fits. A formula solution solves x^2 + x - 2*T <= 0, where T=(240-k)/5, then caps the result by n. Common mistakes: forgetting the cap by n or using floating point without flooring carefully.

Given numbers in a test case, decide whether their sum can be represented as the area of an integer-sided square.
Several test cases with an array of positive integers.
YES if the sum is a perfect square; otherwise NO.
The sum should be handled with integer arithmetic.
[{"note": "Paraphrased demo sample omitted; see source URL."}]
Core condition: exists integer r such that r*r equals the total sum.

Compute the total sum. A formula route uses integer square root and verifies root*root == sum. A binary-search route searches r and compares r*r against the sum. Common mistakes: using imprecise floating point sqrt or overflowing r*r in other languages.

Generate triangular numbers up to n and test complements with a set or binary search. A formula-oriented route tests if m is triangular using discriminant 1+8*m being a perfect square. Common mistakes: not limiting generated triangular numbers or confusing square numbers with triangular numbers.
